title: Union Budget 2024-25 Speech
sourceLabel: Ministry of Finance, Government of India

paragraphs.text: Honourable Speaker Sir, I rise to present the Budget for the financial year 2024-25. This is a budget for Viksit Bharat — a developed India — and it reflects the government's unwavering commitment to inclusive growth, fiscal responsibility, and structural reform. The people of India have entrusted this government with a mandate to deliver prosperity, and this budget is our response to that trust.

The Indian economy has demonstrated extraordinary resilience in a turbulent global environment. Our GDP growth rate of over seven percent makes India the fastest-growing major economy in the world. Inflation has moderated to within the Reserve Bank's target band. Foreign exchange reserves remain robust, and the current account deficit has narrowed significantly. These fundamentals provide a strong platform for the next phase of India's economic transformation.

This government's fiscal consolidation journey has been consistent and credible. The fiscal deficit for 2023-24 is estimated at five point eight percent of GDP, better than the budget estimate. For 2024-25, I am targeting a fiscal deficit of five point one percent of GDP, continuing our glide path toward the medium-term target of four point five percent. Fiscal discipline is not austerity; it is the foundation of macroeconomic stability and sustainable growth.

Sir, employment generation is this budget's foremost priority. We are introducing a comprehensive employment-linked incentive scheme with three components. First, an incentive for first-time employees entering the formal workforce, covering one month's salary up to fifteen thousand rupees through EPFO. Second, incentives for manufacturing sector employers who hire additional workers. Third, support for employers across all sectors who expand their workforce beyond a threshold.

These schemes are designed to formalize employment, bring young Indians into the social security net, and create a virtuous cycle where formal employment generates tax revenue and consumption demand. We estimate these initiatives will benefit over two crore young people and incentivize the creation of substantial new formal sector jobs over the next five years.

Skilling and education are the foundation of a productive workforce. I am announcing a centrally sponsored scheme for skilling twenty lakh youth over five years, aligned with the needs of industry. A new Model Skill Loan scheme will provide institutional credit of up to seven and a half lakh rupees with a government-backed guarantee. We are also establishing one thousand Industrial Training Institutes in hub-and-spoke mode, upgraded with modern courses in AI, robotics, mechatronics, and IoT.

Capital expenditure continues to be the growth engine of this budget. I am allocating eleven lakh eleven thousand one hundred and eleven crore rupees — that is over eleven point one lakh crore — for capital expenditure in 2024-25. This represents a sustained commitment of approximately three point four percent of GDP to building the infrastructure that India needs: roads, railways, airports, ports, digital connectivity, and urban transformation.

For the railways, I am providing a capital outlay of two point five lakh crore rupees. Three major economic railway corridor programmes — energy, mineral and cement corridors, port connectivity corridors, and high-traffic density corridors — will be implemented to enhance logistics efficiency and reduce transportation costs. Forty thousand normal rail bogies will be converted to Vande Bharat standards to improve passenger experience across the network.

The agriculture sector employs nearly half of India's workforce, and its transformation is essential for inclusive growth. I am announcing a comprehensive review of the agricultural research setup to bring focus on higher productivity, climate-resilient crop varieties, and natural farming practices. One crore farmers will be initiated into natural farming, supported by certification and branding to ensure that sustainable agriculture is also profitable agriculture.

For our farmers, I am introducing a digital crop survey in four hundred districts using technology to create a comprehensive database of cropping patterns. This will enable better planning of procurement, insurance, and credit. A challenge fund of crop varieties will support the development of climate-resilient seeds. Storage and post-harvest infrastructure will be strengthened through viability gap funding to reduce crop losses and improve farmer incomes.

Rural development remains a core pillar of this government's agenda. Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana will construct an additional three crore houses in rural and urban areas. The MGNREGA allocation has been enhanced to meet demand in rural areas. A new initiative for saturation of basic amenities — piped water, electricity, LPG, bank accounts, and toilets — will ensure that no household is left behind in the access to essential services.

On tax reform, this budget takes further steps to simplify and rationalize the tax structure. The new tax regime, which is now the default regime, will see an increase in the standard deduction to seventy-five thousand rupees. The tax slabs under the new regime are being restructured to provide additional relief to the middle class. A salaried taxpayer under the new regime can now save up to seventeen thousand five hundred rupees in income tax annually.

On the indirect tax side, I am proposing a comprehensive review of the customs duty structure to rationalize and simplify it. The number of duty rates will be reduced, inverted duty structures will be corrected, and unnecessary exemptions will be phased out. Customs duties on critical minerals essential for electronics, energy transition, and advanced manufacturing will be reduced to zero or minimal levels to strengthen domestic value chains.

The Goods and Services Tax has been a transformational reform, and its continued rationalization remains a priority. GST collections have consistently exceeded expectations, reflecting both the expansion of the tax base and improved compliance. The GST Council will take up further rate rationalization, and the technology platform will be upgraded to improve the taxpayer experience and reduce compliance burden for small businesses.

India's digital public infrastructure — Aadhaar, UPI, and the India Stack — has been a global exemplar. This budget builds on that foundation with new investments in digital governance, AI-driven public service delivery, and a National Data Governance Framework that balances innovation with privacy. The allocation for the Digital India programme has been substantially enhanced to expand digital literacy, connectivity, and e-governance to the last mile.

For the MSME sector, which is the backbone of India's manufacturing and employment, I am enhancing the credit guarantee scheme with increased coverage limits and reduced guarantee fees. A new assessment model based on digital footprint rather than traditional collateral will enable more MSMEs to access institutional credit. SIDBI will open new branches in MSME clusters to provide direct lending and handholding support.

Energy transition is both an environmental imperative and an economic opportunity for India. I am announcing a viability gap funding scheme for offshore wind energy, expanded support for rooftop solar under PM Surya Ghar, and a new initiative for developing pumped storage hydropower projects. India's installed renewable energy capacity will be targeted to reach five hundred gigawatts by 2030, creating millions of green jobs in the process.

Sir, this budget is about building the foundations of a developed India by 2047. Every allocation, every reform, and every initiative is directed toward that goal. We are investing in people — through education, skills, and health. We are investing in infrastructure — physical, digital, and social. We are investing in institutions — strengthening governance, simplifying regulations, and deepening markets. And we are doing all of this within a framework of fiscal responsibility.

I am confident that the Indian economy is on the right trajectory. The reforms of the past decade have laid the groundwork, and the investments we make now will yield dividends for generations to come. With the blessings of this House and the hard work of one hundred and forty crore Indians, we will build a Viksit Bharat — a developed, prosperous, and self-reliant India. Jai Hind.
